title: Citation & Source Attribution
concept: Prompting the model to cite which retrieved chunk supports each claim in its answer.

exercises.prompt: In our last lesson, we learned about 'grounding' the model's answer. What does grounding mean in a RAG system?

options.text: Answering questions using only the provided context documents rather than its internal training data.
Translating the user query into multiple different languages to find the best match.
Formatting the final output as a clean bulleted list for better readability.

exercises.explain: Grounding forces the model to rely strictly on the provided context, preventing it from using general training knowledge (parametric memory) that might be outdated or incorrect.

cards.title: Show Me the Proof!
cards.body: Have you ever read a helpful article online but doubted it because there were no sources? When LLMs answer questions using retrieved data, they can make things up. Citations let users verify exactly where the answers came from.

cards.title: What is Citation?
cards.body: Citation & Source Attribution is the practice of prompting an LLM to link specific parts of its output directly to the retrieved text chunks it used, often using inline tags like [Doc 1] or [Source A].

cards.title: The Open-Book Exam Helper
cards.body: Think of the model as a student taking an open-book exam. Instead of just writing down facts, they must write down the page and paragraph numbers where they found each fact so the teacher can double-check their work.

cards.title: Citations in Action
cards.body: When asked about a company policy, the LLM answers: 'Employees receive 20 days of paid time off [Doc 2], which must be approved by a manager [Doc 5].' This lets the user click [Doc 2] to see the original HR text.

cards.title: The Citation Illusion
cards.body: Many believe asking for citations guarantees the LLM will only use the provided text. In reality, LLMs can still hallucinate citations or link to the wrong source. You must verify citations programmatically.

cards.title: Uncited vs. Cited Answers
cards.body: Adding citations turns a mysterious wall of text into a verifiable, interactive answer where every claim points back to its origin.

left.text: Water boils at lower temperatures at high altitudes.

right.text: Water boils at lower temperatures at high altitudes [Source 3].

exercises.prompt: What is the standard way to prompt an LLM to perform source attribution?

options.text: Tell the model to write out the full URL of the source web page from its memory.
Label each retrieved chunk with an identifier and prompt the model to include that identifier next to the claim it supports.
Ask the model to copy and paste the entire source document at the end of the response.
Allow the model to invent its own citation numbering system freely without labeling the chunks first.

exercises.explain: Asking the model to output specific, structured citation tags (like [1] or [Source 1]) matching the identifiers of the provided chunks makes it easy to parse and link them later.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Once you prompt an LLM to include citations, it is mathematically impossible for it to cite the wrong document.
exercises.explain: Even with citation instructions, models can occasionally reference the wrong source document or hallucinate a non-existent source, so citation checking is still necessary.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing term to complete the best practice for source attribution.
exercises.sentence: To help users verify facts, we should prompt the model to insert inline tags to link claims back to their sources.
exercises.explain: Using inline tags like [Source 1] directly within the text allows users to easily map claims to the correct source documents.

exercises.prompt: Identify which response shows an AI model successfully performing source attribution using retrieved documents.

options.text: The library is open late. I think it closes at 10 PM on weekdays, but you should check their website just in case.
According to a book I read once, the library is closed on Sundays. I don't remember the name of the book.
The library closes at 10 PM on weekdays [Doc 1], but it is closed all day on Sundays [Doc 2].

exercises.explain: The third response is generated by an AI because it outputs factual claims with structured citation tags matching the provided source labels, whereas the others either lack sources or have unstructured text.

exercises.prompt: You are designing a high-reliability RAG system for medical queries. To guarantee that every sentence in the generated summary is backed by real documents, what is the best practice for citation implementation?

options.text: Rely entirely on the LLM's natural honesty, as citation requests completely prevent hallucination.
Ask the LLM to output specific source tags, and then programmatically verify that those tags match the retrieved chunks.
Use a separate LLM call to guess which documents the first LLM used based on semantic similarity.
Only output the final answer text and strip away the citation tags before showing them to the user.

exercises.explain: Requiring the model to return source identifiers (like [Doc 1]) inside its text forces it to verify its claims against specific chunks. However, we must also write code to verify that the cited chunks actually exist in the retrieved document set to prevent the model from hallucinating fake source tags.
